mnemonics: Z-Test Zoo: Z is for Zoo (large sample size), where the normal distribution is the main attraction and the null hypothesis is the animal you're trying to spot.
keyPoints: A test of significance determines if observed sample data deviates significantly from what is expected under the null hypothesis.
For large samples (n > 30), the sampling distribution of many statistics is approximately normal, allowing use of the standard normal (Z) distribution.
The test involves calculating a Z-statistic (e.g., for a mean: Z = (x̄ - μ) / (σ/√n)) and comparing it to a critical value or using a p-value to make a decision.
commonMistakes: Using a Z-test for small samples (n < 30) instead of a t-test, which can lead to inaccurate conclusions.
Misstating the conclusion by saying the test 'proves' the alternative hypothesis; it only provides evidence against the null.
Confusing statistical significance with practical importance; a result can be statistically significant but have a trivial real-world effect.
